title: LDL is not causing heart disease

chunks.title: Introduction to LDL and Atherosclerosis
chunks.summary: The speaker challenges the mainstream belief that apolipoprotein B (ApoB) containing particles like LDL are the primary cause of atherosclerosis. They indicate that research is ongoing, particularly a study by Dave Feldman's group on coronary imaging in patients with elevated LDL levels.
chunks.transcript: this whole thing has gotten very convoluted and because we've come to believe that apob containing particles specifically vldl ldl chylomicrons are the causative agents of atherosclerosis i think this is completely wrong and i will question that now so if ldl is causing atherosclerosis then it sort of makes sense that in humans we should get it as low as possible in all humans dave feldman's group has a really interesting imaging study going on right now there's no data from that study but we will have data in the next few years what they are doing is looking at coronary imaging i believe by ct coronary angiography of patients with quote unquote elevated ldl most of these people are lean mass hyper responders so they have very elevated ldls while remaining insulin sensitive with high hdl low triglycerides this is dave feldman's lipid triad not the lipid triad referenced in this 4s paper that i just mentioned in this podcast but they're following them longitudinally to see if they will develop atherosclerosis most cardiac radiologists say they should because they have elevated ldl we'll wait for the results of that trial

chunks.title: Questioning the Role of LDL
chunks.summary: The discussion shifts to questioning whether LDL is a direct cause of atherosclerosis, suggesting that it might be a result of other underlying issues like endothelial damage. The speaker presents evidence from studies indicating a potential lack of correlation between LDL levels and cardiovascular disease in certain populations.
chunks.transcript: i think it's going to be very very interesting mainstream western medicine physicians believe that because ldl and other apob containing particles are the causative agents of atherosclerosis we should get this as low and as many people as possible i've heard commentary from physicians saying they don't really have a problem with a carnivore or animal-based diet but if someone on an animal-based diet has an elevated ldl they should take a statin to lower that this is essentially i'm paraphrasing but that is true to the spirit of what physicians will say about this way of eating but what if ldl is not causing atherosclerosis what if something else is the first agent of atherosclerosis and ldl is getting wrapped into that process perhaps even being brought there by the body to aid in repair of the vessel wall which has been injured by something else in that case is it possible that in some situations in people who are insulin resistant the majority of the human population on the planet and in this country that elevated ldl sometimes looks worse or sometimes associates with higher rates of cardiovascular disease it's very possible that that is the actual situation that is going on and we can see that if you look at the framingham study much like this 4s study but not involving statins and you look at the relationship between ldl levels low density lipoprotein levels and cardiovascular disease incidents what you find is that if you do that as a whole group and you don't give any sort of third variable to stratify or to break those people out into levels of insulin resistance and insulin sensitivity as ldl increases cardiovascular disease increases this is the type of data that many who believe ldl and apob containing particles are causative of atherosclerosis will point to but they ignore the data that goes further into that analysis and breaks that same set of data from framingham or other trials into subgroups by insulin sensitivity whether you're using fasting insulin whether you're using hdl triglyceride ratio whether you're using isolated hdl what you find is if you break that framingham data into four lines based on your hdl level those who have the highest hdl above 65 milligrams per deciliter essentially show no relationship between levels of ldl and cardiovascular disease while those with the lowest hdl show a very strong relationship suggesting that there is a third variable at play which i would say is clearly insulin resistance aka sensitivity so here is the question that must be answered by anyone who claims that apob is causative of atherosclerosis if that is the case why do we not see atherosclerosis that is the formation of atheromas and plaques in the veins of our body unless they are grafted into the arterial circulation but in a native

chunks.title: Impact of Endothelial Damage
chunks.summary: The speaker highlights the significance of endothelial damage as a precursor to atherosclerosis, emphasizing that it is pressure in arteries, rather than LDL, that leads to lesions. They consider that individuals with insulin resistance and diabetes may experience a higher risk due to their impaired wound healing in arteries.
chunks.transcript: human we do not see atherosclerosis in veins we only see atherosclerosis in arteries why is that because something else is required for the beginning of atheroma and that is damage to the endothelium damage to the inside of the arterial wall damage to the inner layer of blood vessels and in veins veins are so much lower pressure they do not have the damage from the higher pressure in the arterial system which denudes which scrapes off the endothelium in those arteries and leads to a lesion which can become an atheromatous plaque or a fatty streak this is the beginning of the process now what causes the endothelium to get damaged all of our arteries probably experience this even in the healthy individuals at the bifurcations at the sharp corners where you turn the car real sharply the tire screech that creates turbulence even in healthy individuals younger than myself or myself i probably have some damage to my endothelial lining of my arteries at branch points but the normal process of repair in my body i believe repairs those and they do not become atheromas i believe that in diabetics and those who are insulin resistance and we know that these individuals have impaired immunity have impaired wound healing that's why diabetics get amputations of their toes their feet or their legs we know they get retinopathies we know they get all of these sequelae side effects long-term side effects of diabetes because they have impaired immunity and impaired wound healing isn't it likely they also have impaired wound healing in their arterial vascular tree and that the damage to the endothelium which probably is connected with insulin resistance directly is the beginning event of atherosclerosis and then ldl gets wrapped into that and of course if you have damaged endothelium if you have a diabetic individual could higher ldl be worse absolutely absolutely should we try and lower the ldl in that individual who has diabetes and we know has damage endothelium which is not healing properly i would say that's reasonable but let's also correct the root cause of their diabetes in the first place you could give them a stat and i think that's cause of that diabetes i don't think many physicians are because we are not trained to think that way and that is a tragedy that i

chunks.title: Critique of Medical Practices
chunks.summary: The speaker criticizes the medical community for not addressing the root causes of insulin resistance and diabetes, focusing instead on prescribing medications like statins. They stress the need for a shift in how physicians approach treatment, advocating for dietary changes alongside medical intervention.
chunks.transcript: think needs to be changed within our medical system it's something i want to be a part of but i strongly believe that individuals like myself or perhaps you if you're fasting insulin is low and this is not medical advice if your fasting insulin is low i don't think that ldl is in any way shape or form

chunks.title: LDL as a Fireman, Not an Arsonist
chunks.summary: In this segment, the speaker emphasizes their viewpoint that LDL should be viewed as a response to endothelial damage, rather than a direct cause of atherosclerosis. They argue that insufficient evidence supports the idea that elevated LDL directly injures arteries.
chunks.transcript: causative atherosclerosis i think it is the fireman that shows up to put out the fire not the arsonist and i think there is no good evidence that apob directly injures the endothelium if it did why is that same amount of apob that is circulating in the human body not injuring our veins why don't we get atheromas in the veins on previous podcasts i've gone much further down this rabbit hole and talked about glycogen storage disease 1a a condition with elevated levels of ldl with no elevation in rates of atherosclerosis so if april b is causative atherosclerosis how can people with glycogen storage disease 1a not have higher rates of atherosclerosis when they have elevated levels of ldl i've also talked about other conditions which are genetic there's a monogenic form of insulin resistance known as dunnigan familial lipodystrophy where people do not have elevated levels of ldl but they are insulin resistant and they develop massive atherosclerosis i've even talked about in previous podcasts chimp data which shows that chimps have elevated levels of ldl much higher than humans in the 200s but they don't develop atheromas in their arteries the way that humans do but their apob looks just like ours they develop other types of heart disease which are cardiomyopathy and heart failure maybe related to captivity in zoos but they do not develop atheromas in their arteries why then do we believe that ldl that apob is causative of atherosclerosis is there any solid data really pointing really indicting ldl and a b containing particles as directly injuring the endothelium i believe there's not and i believe this is where so many really smart and well-intentioned physicians are being myopic and missing the forest for the trees this is a super important point this is the whole point of this podcast

chunks.title: Comparative Studies and Observations
chunks.summary: The speaker references various studies, including those involving glycogen storage disease and chimpanzees, to argue against the notion that elevated LDL is causative of atherosclerosis. They point out that these subjects do not exhibit the same cardiovascular outcomes as humans despite having high LDL levels.

chunks.title: Conclusion and Call for Reevaluation
chunks.summary: The speaker concludes by urging that more nuanced views on LDL and atherosclerosis are necessary, confronting institutional mindsets that might overlook critical aspects of cardiovascular health. They emphasize the importance of evidence-based examination of LDL's role in heart diseases.
chunks.transcript: smart and well-intentioned physicians are being myopic and missing the forest for the trees this is a super important point this is the whole point of this
